Monte-Carlo Neural Approximation with a Physics-Informed Neural Network

  • 10 March 2026

Article en cours d'écriture.

We use CUDA to generate european call option pricings in the VG model with a Monte-Carlo simulation. We then wrote python bindings to use torch to train a model to replicate the MC simulation results using data generated on-the-fly.

Code : https://github.com/fntb/vg-model-nn-approximation

Notes on the Loss

Given a random variable xxx dependant on θ\thetaθ, our goal is to regress μ(θ):=E[x∣θ]\mu(\theta) := \mathbb{E}[x \vert \theta]μ(θ):=E[x∣θ].

We have access to a dataset { xj∣θi }\set{x_j \vert \theta_i}{xj​∣θi​} with i∈{ 1…n }i \in \set{1 \dots n}i∈{1…n} and j∈{ 1…m }j \in \set{1 \dots m}j∈{1…m}. We assume that for each iii { xj∣θi }j\set{x_j \vert \theta_i}_j{xj​∣θi​}j​ are i.i.di.i.di.i.d ; and that { θi }i\set{\theta_i}_i{θi​}i​ are i.i.di.i.di.i.d too.

For all iii, we write μ^(θi):=∑j=1mxj∣θi\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) := \sum_{j = 1}^m x_j \vert \theta_iμ^​(θi​):=∑j=1m​xj​∣θi​ the conditional empirical mean and σ^(θi)\hat{\sigma}(\theta_i)σ^(θi​) the (conditional) empirical variance.

Suppose that mmm is large, and - motivated by the TCL, LGN and Slutsky's theorem - assume that :

m(μ^(θ)−μ(θ))σ^(θ)2∼N(0,1)\sqrt{m} \frac{(\hat{\mu}(\theta) - \mu(\theta))}{\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2}} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) m​σ^(θ)2​(μ^​(θ)−μ(θ))​∼N(0,1)

Condition by σ^(θ)2\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2σ^(θ)2 and rewrite :

μ^(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),σ^(θ)2m)\hat{\mu}(\theta) \vert \hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(\theta), \frac{\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2}{m}) μ^​(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),mσ^(θ)2​)

Note that { θi }i\set{\theta_i}_i{θi​}i​ being supposed i.i.di.i.di.i.d, { μ^(θi)∣σ^(θi)2 }i\set{\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) \vert \hat{\sigma}(\theta_i)^2}_i{μ^​(θi​)∣σ^(θi​)2}i​ are too which justifies the use of the likelihood.

So we then have the negative conditional log-likelihood :

−log⁡∏i=1np(μ^(θi)∣σ^(θi)2)∝∑i=1n(μ^(θi)−μ(θi))22σ^(θi)2/m- \log{\prod_{i = 1}^n p(\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) \vert \hat{\sigma}(\theta_i)^2)} \propto \sum_{i = 1}^n \frac{(\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) - \mu(\theta_i))^2}{2 \hat{\sigma}(\theta_i)^2 / m} −logi=1∏n​p(μ^​(θi​)∣σ^(θi​)2)∝i=1∑n​2σ^(θi​)2/m(μ^​(θi​)−μ(θi​))2​

And minimizing this criteria will give us the MLE.

Linking to the Loss Function Choice in the case of Approximating a Monte-Carlo simulation using a Neural Network

Now to put this in context, we have a stochastic function fff and a dataset of parameters { θi }\set{\theta_i}{θi​}, we want to learn a parametric function μϕ\mu_\phiμϕ​ (i.e. a neural network) to approximate μ:θ→E[f(θ)]\mu : \theta \to \mathbb{E}[f(\theta)]μ:θ→E[f(θ)].

To accomplish this we generate, for each θi\theta_iθi​, mmm simulations { f(θi)j }j\set{f(\theta_i)_j}_j{f(θi​)j​}j​ (the xj∣θix_j\vert \theta_ixj​∣θi​ in our previous explanation) of which we take the empirical mean μ^(θi)\hat{\mu}(\theta_i)μ^​(θi​) (i.e. the monte-carlo simulation of E[f(θi)]\mathbb{E}[f(\theta_i)]E[f(θi​)]) and empirical variance σ^(θi)2\hat{\sigma}(\theta_i)^2σ^(θi​)2.

We can then train our neural network by gradient descent on the MLE objective (i.e. the aforementionned NLL, which is in the end a simple weighted MSE) :

WMSE⁡(μϕ(θi),μ^(θi)∣σ^2(θi),m)=∑i=1n(μ^(θi)−μϕ(θi))2σ^2(θi)/m\operatorname{WMSE}(\mu_\phi(\theta_i), \hat{\mu}(\theta_i) \vert \hat{\sigma}^2(\theta_i), m) = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{(\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) - \mu_\phi(\theta_i))^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2(\theta_i) / m} WMSE(μϕ​(θi​),μ^​(θi​)∣σ^2(θi​),m)=i=1∑n​σ^2(θi​)/m(μ^​(θi​)−μϕ​(θi​))2​

Handling Floating Point Precision Errors

We face a last issue, as we assumed mmm to be large, the Monte-Carlo estimate may be too good, and the variance σ^(θ)2\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2σ^(θ)2 may be very close to 000 which would cause NaN issues in the previous objective function since we divide by σ^(θ)2\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2σ^(θ)2.

To edge against this we can model a noise component interpreted as the machine (floating point) precision. Instead of assuming

μ^(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),σ^(θ)2m)\hat{\mu}(\theta) \vert \hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(\theta), \frac{\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2}{m}) μ^​(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),mσ^(θ)2​)

Assume that :

μ^(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),σ^(θ)2m)+N(0,ϵ)\hat{\mu}(\theta) \vert \hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2 \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(\theta), \frac{\hat{\sigma}(\theta)^2}{m}) + \mathcal{N}(0, \epsilon) μ^​(θ)∣σ^(θ)2∼N(μ(θ),mσ^(θ)2​)+N(0,ϵ)

We get :

WMSE⁡(μϕ(θi),μ^(θi)∣σ^2(θi),m)=∑i=1n(μ^(θi)−μϕ(θi))2σ^2(θi)/m+ϵ\operatorname{WMSE}(\mu_\phi(\theta_i), \hat{\mu}(\theta_i) \vert \hat{\sigma}^2(\theta_i), m) = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{(\hat{\mu}(\theta_i) - \mu_\phi(\theta_i))^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2(\theta_i) / m + \epsilon} WMSE(μϕ​(θi​),μ^​(θi​)∣σ^2(θi​),m)=i=1∑n​σ^2(θi​)/m+ϵ(μ^​(θi​)−μϕ​(θi​))2​

Conclusion

  • The weighted MSE is not a default choice, but instead is justified as a proper Maximum Likelihood Estimation ;
  • Our only two hypothesis where "ppp is large" - which it is at 272^727 - and "conditionned on { m^(θ),σ^(θ) }\set{\hat{m}(\theta), \hat{\sigma}(\theta)}{m^(θ),σ^(θ)}" - which translates to "Assuming the MC estimate is the true parameter".
